title: Ruth E. Carter: Oscar-Winning Costume Designer's Career
text: Ruth E. Carter, a Springfield native, has become the most-nominated Black woman in Oscar history. Her fifth nomination comes for her costume design work on the film Sinners, which received a record-breaking 16 Oscar nominations this year. In 2019, Carter made history as the first Black costume designer to win the Academy Award for best costume design for Black Panther. With her latest nomination for Sinners, she has cemented her legacy in Hollywood, boasting over 70 credits across television, theater, and film. The supernatural horror film is set in Jim Crow-era Mississippi and centers on sharecroppers and the origins of blues music. Rather than embracing traditional vampire aesthetics, Carter prioritized historical authenticity. She explained: Fashion doesn't evolve evenly. I kind of played with the era. Carter conducted detailed research on sharecropper workwear, noting elements like cuffed hems on overalls that indicated hand-me-down garments. For Michael B. Jordan's dual roles as twins Smoke and Stack, she used costume details particularly hats to convey their distinct personalities. Carter credits her Massachusetts foundation for her artistic development. She learned to sew at her neighborhood Boys & Girls Club and participated in theater programs at local universities before attending Hampton University in Virginia. Her early career breakthrough came through collaborations with filmmaker Spike Lee.
